data.title: U.K. government bailout saves Vestas turbine factory set for closure
data.description:
The U.K. government said Wednesday it will provide a £20M (~$27.5B) grant to Vestas Wind's (VWDRY) Isle of Wight wind turbine plant that had been slated for closure but will now be converted to supply blades for onshore developments.

The Vestas (VWDRY) facility had faced an uncertain future after demand ended for the offshore blades it produced, and logistical constraints prevented production of larger next-generation turbines at the site.

The grant will help the company repurpose the site to develop onshore wind turbines and secure the future of the factory, the government said.

"This will rebuild domestic manufacturing, strengthen energy security, and support growth in onshore wind, which is needed to end the UK's dependency on volatile fossil fuel markets," Britain's Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said in a statement.

data.title: Bridgewater Bancshares targets achieving 3% net interest margin by end of 2026 while expanding affordable housing vertical
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bridgewater Bancshares, Inc. (BWB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Gerald Baack, CEO, outlined a strong finish to the year, citing "robust loan and core deposit growth, net interest margin expansion and higher fee income." He emphasized that "expenses were also well controlled and asset quality remained strong," attributing the quarter’s success to the Bridgewater Bank team and their ongoing ability to take market share through an "unconventional reliable experience." Expansion in the Twin Cities and outside the market, particularly in affordable housing, was highlighted as a growth driver. Baack also mentioned closing one of the two branches acquired via First Minnetonka City Bank and plans to open a new branch in Lake Elmo next month. He stated, "Bridgewater is now the second largest locally led bank in the Twin Cities."
  • Baack addressed market dynamics, noting talent and client opportunities from local M&A activity and reaffirmed the company's market positioning. He acknowledged recent community challenges in the Twin Cities and expressed support for affected team members and clients.
  • Joseph Chybowski, President & CFO, stated, "We expected net interest margin expansion to return in the fourth quarter given 3 Fed rate cuts in late 2025. And this is exactly what happened as the margin increased 12 basis points to 2.75%, primarily due to lower deposit costs." Chybowski further indicated, "Given the expansion we saw in the fourth quarter and as we look ahead to repricing opportunities in 2026, we are actually pulling forward and believe we can get to 3% NIM by the end of 2026, and this does not assume any additional rate cuts."
  • Nicholas Place, Chief Banking Officer, highlighted "annualized core deposit growth of 8.8% in the fourth quarter and 7.9% for the full year of 2025," and a $100 million increase in noninterest-bearing deposits during the quarter. Place also noted that affordable housing balances increased $41 million in the quarter or 27% annualized.

Outlook

  • Management now projects reaching a 3% net interest margin by the end of 2026, accelerated from the previous guidance of early 2027. Chybowski said, "We think we can now get to a 3% net interest margin by the end of 2026 instead of early 2027, and this does not assume any additional rate cuts."
  • Loan growth is expected to remain in the "high single digits in 2026," contingent on core deposit growth and loan payoff levels.
  • Expense growth is projected to align with asset growth, reverting to historical patterns following the systems conversion, with Chybowski stating, "We expect to get back to growing expenses in line with assets, unlike 2025, where expense growth was a bit higher."

Financial Results

  • Net interest margin expanded by 12 basis points to 2.75%, attributed to lower deposit costs, which declined by 22 basis points to 2.97% in Q4.
  • Net interest income increased 5% during the quarter, driven by margin expansion and earning asset growth.
  • Noninterest income rebounded with $651,000 in swap fee income, after no swap fee income in Q3. Total revenue increased 32% year-over-year.
  • Core deposit growth of 9% and loan growth of 9% were reported for the quarter.
  • Tangible book value grew 16.5% annualized and was up 15.3% year-over-year.
  • Adjusted efficiency ratio improved to 50.7%, the lowest since Q1 2023.
  • Allowance ratio declined slightly to 1.31% due to a $1.2 million charge-off, but management characterized these credit events as isolated.
  • CET1 ratio increased to 9.17%.

Q&A

  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler, inquired about the seasonality of deposit growth and the impact of recent hires. Nicholas Place responded that Q4 tends to be a high watermark due to client behavior, with "balances late in the year and some of those balances do trickle out in Q1." He affirmed confidence in the deposit pipeline, noting seasonality but expressing readiness to leverage broker deposits if required.
  • Race asked about the cadence of loan repricing. Chybowski stated, "It's pretty well laid out... continued repricing higher. That will be the biggest driver the NIM guide to 3% is really on the asset side."
  • Race queried expense outlook. Chybowski confirmed "high singles" for expense growth, in line with asset growth, and Place added that operational leverage will come from both staff capacity and process improvements, with opportunistic hiring continuing.
  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group, asked about the impact of potential additional rate cuts on margin. Chybowski explained that "if you do get kind of an implied rate scenario and 2 cuts this year, that just pulls that forward and directly impacts deposit costs."
  • Nosal inquired about asset quality regarding a CBD office loan. Katie Morrell said, "We expect that to be a longer-term workout... we still have a specific reserve on the loan."
  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson, queried the scale of the affordable housing vertical. Place responded it is "roughly 15 or so percent of the book today overall" and expected to "increase as a percentage of the book overall here near term."
  • Rulis asked about swap fees, and Place indicated that swap fee revenue is expected to moderate but remain a portion of revenue.
  • Brandon Rud, Stephens, asked about the timeline for core banking system modernization. Chybowski replied that it's "a longer-term initiative" aimed at both internal efficiency and client experience.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on growth sustainability, margin outlook, and risk management, with Race and Nosal probing for details on execution and risk. Questions were direct and sought clarification on projections and specific loan exposures.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone during both prepared remarks and Q&A. Baack and Chybowski used phrases such as "we are very optimistic," "we feel really good," and "we are actually pulling forward" guidance, reflecting increased confidence compared to the previous quarter.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more bullish, particularly regarding the timeline for achieving the 3% net interest margin. Analysts remained consistently focused on key growth and risk themes without marked shifts in skepticism or concern.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for reaching a 3% net interest margin was accelerated from early 2027 to the end of 2026, reflecting greater management confidence.
  • Loan and core deposit growth remained robust, with high single-digit expectations reiterated for 2026.
  • Noninterest income rebounded due to swap fees after a decline in Q3.
  • Expense growth is now expected to align more closely with asset growth, as opposed to the higher expense growth in Q3 due to the systems conversion.
  • Management’s tone is more assertive regarding profitability and margin expansion, while analysts' focus on risk and growth drivers continued without major shifts.

Risks and Concerns

  • Modest uptick in nonperforming assets and net charge-offs was noted, but management characterized these as isolated and not indicative of systemic issues.
  • Continued competition in the Twin Cities is impacting loan and deposit pricing. Place stated, "We definitely saw increased competition, particularly on the loan front throughout 2025."
  • Exposure to the office sector remains limited and is being actively managed, with one CBD office loan in a long-term workout.
  • Deposit growth seasonality and reliance on broker deposits if core deposits lag were highlighted as factors to monitor.

Final Takeaway

Management at Bridgewater Bancshares underscored robust execution in Q4 2025, with accelerated guidance to achieve a 3% net interest margin by the end of 2026, strong growth in both loans and core deposits, and expanding revenue streams from areas such as affordable housing. The leadership team emphasized a disciplined approach to expense growth and risk management, with a positive outlook for continued market share gains and profitability in the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Stifel Financial outlines $6B–$6.35B revenue target for 2026 as margins improve and recruiting momentum builds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Stifel Financial Corp. (SF) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Ronald J. Kruszewski, Chairman & CEO, began by stating, "2025 was another record year for Stifel. Firm-wide revenue of $5.5 billion increased 11% and marked the first time we've surpassed $5 billion in revenue in our 135-year history." He highlighted record performance in Global Wealth Management and the second highest year of institutional revenue, emphasizing the breadth and resilience of the franchise.

  • Kruszewski underscored Stifel's client-centric business model, noting, "Approximately 80% of our revenue comes from wealth management, asset management, investment banking and capital markets, with net interest income representing about 20% of the mix." He indicated that the balance sheet is used to support client needs, not as a standalone business line.

  • The CEO pointed to significant capital actions: "The Board of Directors authorized an 11% increase in the common stock dividend beginning in the first quarter of 2026. In addition, the Board authorized a 3-for-2 stock split effective February 26, 2026." He also referenced the acquisition of Brian Gardner and the employee wealth business from B. Riley.

  • James Marischen, Senior VP & CFO, stated, "Revenue was a record $1.56 billion, surpassing last quarter's record by 9% with both operating segments delivering solid results." He continued, "The performance across both segments drove record EPS of $2.63, a pretax margin of more than 22% and a return on tangible equity of more than 31%." Marischen highlighted the sale of Stifel Independent Advisors as a move to improve operating leverage.

Outlook

  • Kruszewski outlined, "Total net revenue is expected to be in range of $6 billion to $6.35 billion. I would note that this reflects the impact of the SIA sale and the closing of our European equities business, which together represented $100 million of annual revenue." He explained that guidance excludes these revenues but expects improved expenses and margins to offset the change.

  • Net interest income is forecasted to be between $1.1 billion and $1.2 billion, supported by approximately $4 billion of balance sheet growth. The compensation ratio is now in the range of 56.5% to 57.5% and non-compensation operating ratio is 18% to 20%.

Financial Results

  • The firm delivered record quarterly revenue of $1.56 billion, and Global Wealth Management achieved revenue of $933 million for the quarter. Institutional revenue reached $610 million, up 28% year-over-year. EPS came in at $2.63, with a pretax margin above 22% and return on tangible equity exceeding 31%.

  • The company ended the quarter with record total client assets of $552 billion and record fee-based assets of $225 million. Recruiting added 181 financial advisers in 2025, including 92 experienced advisers with trailing 12-month production of $86 million.

  • Net interest income for the first quarter of 2026 is expected to range from $275 million to $285 million. The Tier 1 leverage ratio increased to 11.4%, and the Tier 1 risk-based capital ratio rose to 18.3%. Stifel repurchased 335,000 shares during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Michael Brown, UBS: Asked about recruitment factors for 2026 and productivity expansion from new advisers, especially those from B. Riley. Kruszewski responded, "I, for sure, have noticed a productivity increase in B. Riley, now some of that's market, but a lot of it is just platform technology products." He added, "Recruiting strong. If anything, I'm thinking about even increasing our allocation to recruiting because I think our platform and where we are, allows us to really gain even more market share if we choose."

  • Brown followed up on institutional deal momentum. Kruszewski noted, "We see an increase now in activity in health care. Health care at one point was one of our largest sectors, and we're seeing equity capital markets transactions and other transactions in health care."

  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe Research: Inquired about ECM share gains and comp guidance. Kruszewski explained, "We are moving up in our participation levels, and we're doing more deals that go to the -- just the level of capability that we brought to the farm." Marischen provided details on cost savings from the SIA sale and European reorganization.

  • Devin Ryan, Citizens JMP Securities: Sought perspective on the adviser mix industry trend and net interest income assumptions. Kruszewski suggested a slowing shift to the independent channel, while Marischen elaborated on expected balance sheet growth and funding strategy.

  • Brennan Hawken, BMO: Asked about C&I loan growth and private credit markets. Marischen attributed asset yield changes to fee income variability and portfolio reclassification, while Kruszewski stated, "Our CLO book and very, very little exposure to some of the names... we're not seeing any issues there."

  • William Katz, TD Cowen: Queried on strategic use of capital and margin drivers. Kruszewski said, "We increased the dividend, right? So the dividend is up 11%." He emphasized a selective approach to M&A and capital deployment.

  • Y. Cho, JPMorgan: Focused on bank M&A runway and recruiting spending. Kruszewski said, "I think that the banking industry is in a period of consolidation," and mentioned potentially increasing recruiting efforts.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst questions focused on sustainability of recruiting momentum, sector diversification, margin improvement, and strategic capital allocation, with a generally constructive but probing tone.

  • Management maintained a confident and forward-looking stance, with Kruszewski saying, "I feel that the firm and it's our capabilities and our ability to grow from here, frankly, never been better."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone remained constructive but more focused on execution and the impact of recent structural changes, while management's tone shifted from emphasizing market optimism to highlighting operational discipline and growth visibility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw increased guidance specificity, including a $6 billion to $6.35 billion revenue target and explicit margin and expense ratio ranges. The previous quarter emphasized optimism around market environment and strategic flexibility but lacked such numerical guidance.

  • Strategic focus has shifted towards improving operating leverage, with the sale of Stifel Independent Advisors and the closing of the European equities business representing material structural changes.

  • Analysts' questions this quarter concentrated more on execution, capital allocation, and the sustainability of recruiting and margin improvements. Management displayed higher confidence, referencing record adviser recruiting, robust pipelines, and increased capital returns, while reiterating long-term growth ambitions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing market volatility, geopolitical risk, and policy uncertainty. Kruszewski stated, "We remain mindful that risks are ever present that the market conditions can change quickly."

  • Analysts probed into private credit market risks, sustainability of loan spreads, and the competitive landscape for adviser recruiting. Management highlighted a conservative credit risk profile and strong capital position as mitigants.

Final Takeaway

Stifel Financial closed 2025 with record results and enters 2026 projecting total net revenue of $6 billion to $6.35 billion, supported by robust adviser recruiting, expanding institutional pipelines, and disciplined cost management. Management emphasized a constructive business environment, enhanced capital flexibility, and a focus on further improving operating leverage following recent business exits and organizational changes, reinforcing confidence in the firm’s ability to achieve its long-term growth objectives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. stocks retreat from highs as traders await Fed decision, big tech earnings amid geopolitical turmoil
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were higher on Wednesday, with the S&P 500 ((SP500)) touching on the 7,000 level in new intraday highs before paring some gains, as investors awaited the Federal Reserve's decision on interest rates and mega-cap tech company earnings.

This came as U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to attack Iran with a “massive armada” if the country does not agree to a deal over nuclear weapons.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) momentarily touched on the 7,000 mark, adding to current highs, and was last -0.1% in midday trade. The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.1%, and the Dow (DJI) was flat.

“A massive Armada is heading to Iran… It is, ready, willing, and able to rapidly fulfill its mission, with speed and violence, if necessary… Don’t make that happen again,” President Trump said on Truth Social, earlier on Wednesday, referencing a previous military operation he called “Operation Midnight Hammer.”

Traders are also awaiting the Fed's decision this afternoon on interest rates, which are expected to remain at the current rate of 3.5%-3.75%.

“The big question isn’t what the U.S. Federal Reserve will do with rates—the market has priced the probability of a change (i.e., cut) at a de minimis 3%. Instead, the focus is on how central bankers characterize the economic outlook and policy implications,” said Mohamed El-Erian, chief economic adviser at Allianz.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) continued reaching new highs, surpassing $5,300 per ounce on Wednesday, while silver (XAGUSD:CUR) reached the $115 mark.

Meanwhile, the U.S. dollar (DXY) fell to its lowest level in almost four years due to geopolitical risks, intervention in the Japanese yen, and the Fed’s interest rate decision.

In addition, major tech company earnings will arrive postmarket, including Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), and Tesla (TSLA).

Over in the bond market, U.S. Treasury yields were virtually unchanged, with the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) 1 basis point higher at 4.26%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) was flat at 3.59%.

“The bond market, precious metals, and currencies seem on edge…there’s a bit of déjà vu with regards to the interplay of the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y), the dollar index (DXY), and gold (XAUUSD:CUR),” said Jurrien Timmer, director of global macro at Fidelity.

“Tariffs or not, the world order is changing from a dollar-only standard to multiple spheres of influence, and the markets are taking notice. In fact, one could argue that all commodities are becoming strategic assets in this multipolar world,” he added.

data.title: Top performing utilities stocks in the past month
data.description:

data.title: Carvana drops on short report from Gotham City Research
data.description:
Carvana (CVNA) fell 8.3% as Gotham City Research released a short report. 

Gotham City disclosed that it has a short position in Carvana (CVNA).

Carvana didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's email request for comment. 

Carvana (CVNA) has short interest of 11.4%.

data.title: ASML outlines €34B–€39B 2026 revenue target amid record Q4, EUV momentum, and major engineering restructuring
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Christophe Fouquet, President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management, highlighted a “record quarter, record year, record booking,” attributing the surge to strengthening AI-driven demand across both advanced logic and memory. Fouquet stated, “The last 3 months have really clarified basically at least the horizon for 2026 and most probably a bit beyond that.” He emphasized that “AI will have also this effect basically to really drive our entire product portfolio in the coming years.”
  • Fouquet noted that 2026 is positioned as a “big year for EUV,” with increased shipments expected. The CEO also discussed the acceleration of lithography intensity, citing that “the number of layers of EUV increasing basically at our key customer,” which signals a greater need for advanced lithography tools.
  • The CEO confirmed long-term targets remain unchanged: “We still expect for 2030 revenue between EUR 44 billion and EUR 60 billion with a gross margin of 56% to 60%.”
  • R.J.M. Dassen, Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board, stated, “Q4 2025, a record quarter by any standard. It was a record quarter in terms of sales. It was a record quarter in terms of order intake. It was a record quarter in terms of cash flow generation.” Dassen specified, “We ended the year with EUR 32.7 billion in net revenue, 52.8% gross margin… net income of EUR 9.6 billion and an EPS earnings per share of close to EUR 25 per ordinary share.”
  • Dassen highlighted that EUV revenue grew 39% year-on-year, driven by a mix of more tools, higher sales prices, and the “recognition of a number of EXE tools, High NA tools.”
  • Fouquet announced a restructuring of the technology organization, resulting in 1,700 job cuts primarily among leadership roles, to “really boost again our engineering capability, our innovation engine.” He explained, “Out of the 3,000 people that we don't need basically to lead the team, we are going to create 1,400 engineering positions.”

Outlook

  • Dassen guided for first quarter 2026 net revenue between EUR 8.2 billion and EUR 8.9 billion, with a gross margin between 51% and 53%.
  • For the full year 2026, net revenue is expected between EUR 34 billion and EUR 39 billion, representing a midpoint growth of 12% over 2025.
  • Dassen stated, “We once again expect the EUV business to go up significantly this year. We also expect the installed base business to go up this year, and it will go a little bit at the detriment of the non-EUV business.”
  • Management anticipates the China business will decrease to around 20% of total sales in 2026.

Financial Results

  • ASML reported EUR 32.7 billion in net revenue for 2025, with a gross margin of 52.8% and net income of EUR 9.6 billion. Earnings per share were close to EUR 25 per ordinary share.
  • EUV was confirmed as the main driver, with 39% growth year-on-year. Leading technologies (EUV and immersion) accounted for 90% of systems revenue.
  • The installed base business reached EUR 8.2 billion in 2025, nearly matching ASML’s total 2017 revenue.
  • Dassen noted a 26% increase in installed base business, attributing the growth to service and upgrades, particularly for EUV tools.
  • Deep UV revenue declined 6%, with most of the decrease attributed to China.

Q&A

  • Sarah Jacob, Bloomberg News, inquired about restructuring costs. Dassen answered, “These costs in the grand scheme of ASML would not be considered material.”
  • Jacob also questioned the sustainability of capacity expansion. Fouquet explained, “We have seen our customer basically moving forward. They start to really believe in the sustainability of the AI demand… and as a result, they started to invest.”
  • Financial Times asked about AI memory shortage. Fouquet responded, “The demand for high bandwidth memory, which is the AI memory is extremely high. But the demand for DDR memory… is also very high. And as a result, we have seen basically the price of DRAM going up significantly in the last few weeks.”
  • An analyst asked about the impact of the stabilizing AI market on job growth in Eindhoven. Fouquet indicated, “The long-term trajectory is still a trajectory of growth… we will be adding jobs in manufacturing and customer service.”
  • Mark [NSA] asked about internal structure changes and supply chain readiness. Fouquet clarified, “The transformation… will be mostly around D&E… everything else is connected to that.” Dassen added, “We have a very solid understanding also with our supply chain. They're doing the same thing.”
  • Chemical Daily inquired about R&D spending split. Dassen stated, “The lion's share of the R&D expense really is an EUV, right?”
  • An analyst queried about the China business decline. Dassen replied, “It is going down in absolute numbers as well… it goes down in system sales, I think, something like EUR 850 million… but it is reduced in comparison to what it was last year. So it's more normalization than that anything very spectacular is going on there.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on sustainability of AI-driven demand, restructuring impact, and China market normalization, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as most concerns were directly addressed.
  • Management tone was confident in prepared remarks, highlighting “record” achievements and growth potential. In Q&A, responses remained composed, with Fouquet describing the restructuring as “most probably the most difficult decision the management team ever had to make in ASML,” and Dassen repeatedly stating, “We are very well prepared.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, current sentiment is more optimistic on AI-related demand and business visibility, with less emphasis on uncertainty.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter marked a shift from uncertainty to clarity in business outlook, as Fouquet noted more defined demand for 2026 and beyond, specifically from AI-driven investment.
  • The restructuring of the technology organization was a significant new announcement, compared with last quarter’s management appointments and succession planning.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from visibility and booking linearity to the practical impacts of record demand and organizational changes.
  • Key metrics such as net revenue, EUV growth, and installed base business all rose, while the China sales percentage and Deep UV revenue declined versus last quarter’s projections.
  • Management’s confidence level increased, reflecting stronger order intake and industry tailwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited organizational complexity as a challenge, prompting the decision to cut 1,700 leadership positions to boost engineering productivity.
  • Dassen acknowledged dependency on customer capacity readiness and supply chain coordination to meet rising demand.
  • The normalization of China revenue remains a risk, with Dassen suggesting a continued decrease as the backlog is worked through.
  • Energy cost and availability were flagged as long-term industry risks, more so than precious metal pricing.

Final Takeaway

ASML management underscores that record Q4 results and full-year performance were propelled by surging AI-driven demand, with a robust outlook for 2026 supported by a projected revenue range of EUR 34 billion to EUR 39 billion. The company is executing a significant restructuring to reinforce its engineering and innovation capabilities, while anticipating sustained growth in EUV and installed base businesses. Management maintains confidence in its long-term targets and capacity for continued expansion, despite planned reductions in leadership roles and ongoing normalization in the China market.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Chipotle brings viral sensation Chicken al Pastor back to its menu
data.description:
Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) plans to bring back Chicken al Pastor as a limited-time protein menu item on February 10 across restaurants in the U.S., Canada, the U.K., France, and Germany. A promotion will see Chipotle Rewards members receiving early digital access on February 9. 

The menu item features marinated chicken seasoned with seared morita peppers, ground achiote, and pineapple juice, finished with lime and cilantro to deliver a bold, tangy, slightly spicy flavor profile. Notably, Chipotle's (CMG) Chicken al Pastor menu option has built a strong fan following since its initial launch as the chain's first global innovation dish. Positioned as the brand’s most-requested limited-time offering, Chicken al Pastor has generated roughly twice as many consumer requests for a comeback as any other Chipotle limited-time offer, due in part to strong social media engagement that can be activated quickly through targeted marketing and loyalty program communication. 

Chipotle (CMG) executives have credited Chicken al Pastor with boosting incremental transactions and contributing to positive traffic trends in the past, suggesting that its return could be a boost for second-quarter earnings results. Analysts noted that premium proteins typically carry higher price points for CMG, meaning the item is likely to support average check growth and comparable sales even if traffic gains are modest.

Chipotle (CMG) last offered Chicken al Pastor in spring 2024 as a limited-time item, following its wider rollouts in 2023 after initial market tests in 2022.

data.title: The Fed should not ignore the inflation target – Fmr. Kansas City Fed president
data.description:
Former Kansas City Fed President Esther George said she firmly opposes the Federal Reserve’s last rate cuts and maintains that the central bank cannot afford to overlook its inflation target.

In an interview with CNBC, George reiterated that she would not have voted for the 25-basis-point cut in December, emphasizing that with inflation running well above target for nearly five years, the Fed must remain vigilant.

“The truth is that the inflation target cannot be ignored,” George stated.

The former Fed official acknowledged that the committee has signaled a pause after implementing three rate cuts, noting their stated concern about supporting the labor market. However, she stressed that the inflation landscape will be shaped by numerous economic movements in the year ahead that warrant close attention.

George said she would be watching how the Fed judges the risk—whether it sees a “two-sided risk” that justifies a wait-and-see approach.

Sentiment surveys from both consumers and businesses are sending clear signals about economic anxiety. “High prices are a problem. People don’t like high prices,” she said, adding that the combination of elevated prices and limited hiring activity has soured public sentiment even as the broader economy appears to be moving forward.

When asked about proposals to cut rates to improve consumer affordability through cheaper car loans and mortgages, George cautioned against such intervention. “There is a high cost of good intentions as well,” she warned, explaining that the cost would come in the form of creating more demand when inflation is already elevated.

George argued that stimulating additional demand through rate cuts would ultimately become “an attack against the purchasing power of the very consumer that you are trying to help.”

Rather than heavy intervention, she advocated for allowing market dynamics to play out naturally.

The long-term remedy, according to George, requires calibrating interest rate policy to bring supply and demand into balance. Once equilibrium is achieved, the economy can grow without consumers constantly worrying about prices—a concern that was largely absent for more than two decades but has now become a dominant factor in how Americans view their purchasing power and standard of living.

More on interest rates

  • S&P 500 tops 7,000 level on Fed day, big tech earnings amid Iran 'massive armada' turmoil
  • U.S. economy gone from slowdown to worries about overacceleration – Apollo Global’s Torsten Slok
  • Orrstown outlines 5% loan growth target for 2026 while expanding fee income and capital investments
  • Fed rate decision preview: Markets expect a hold, but who dissents?

data.title: Top performing healthcare stocks in the past month
data.description:
As the first month of 2026 comes to an end this week and earnings season accelerates, below is a list of the top 10 healthcare stocks with market capitalizations of $10B or more, ranked by their one-month price performance percentage.

The list includes companies operating across various healthcare industries, such as Biotechnology, Life Sciences Tools and Services, Health Care Equipment, Health Care Services, and Pharmaceuticals.

The list is topped by Moderna (MRNA), which showed an impressive 45.67% one-month performance. Revolution Medicines (RVMD) and Revvity (RVTY) follow, with strong performance percentages of 23.62% and 18.46%, respectively.

Among the top 10 performers, two stocks carry particularly strong quantitative recommendations. Gilead Sciences (GILD) and Guardant Health (GH) both currently hold a Quant Rating of Strong Buy, with scores of 4.61 and 4.88, respectively.

Here is the list:

  1. Moderna (MRNA), 1 month performance percentage: 45.67%
